Sure, I can assist you with coding on TradingView. What specific code or project do you need help with?
i wanna exit pric = live price

Okay, to exit a position when the live price meets a specific condition, you can use the "strategy.exit" function. Here's an example of how to exit a long position when the live price reaches a certain level:

```
//@version=4

strategy("Exit on Live Price", overlay=true)

// Set up long entry condition
longCondition = crossover(sma(close, 20), sma(close, 50))

// Enter long position
strategy.entry("Long", true, when=longCondition)

// Exit long position when live price equals a specific price
exitPrice = 12000 // Change to your desired exit price
if (strategy.position_size > 0 && close >= exitPrice)
    strategy.exit("Long Exit", "Long", comment="Exit at specific price")
```
In the example above, we first set up a long entry condition using a crossover of the 20-period and 50-period simple moving averages.

Then, when the long entry condition is met, we enter a long position using the `strategy.entry` function.

Finally, we use an `if` statement to check if we have a long position open and if the live price is greater than or equal to the `exitPrice`. If both conditions are true, we exit the long position using the `strategy.exit` function. 

You can adjust the `exitPrice` variable to your desired exit price.
what i mean exit pric = live price on market

To exit a position at the current market price, you can use the `strategy.close` function with the argument `market` set to `true`. 

Here's an example script that closes a long position at the current market price:
```
//@version=4
strategy("Exit Long at Market Price", overlay=true)

// Set up long entry condition
longCondition = crossover(sma(close, 20), sma(close, 50))

// Enter long position
strategy.entry("Long", strategy.long, when=longCondition)

// Close long position at market price
if (strategy.position_size > 0)
    strategy.close("Long", comment="Exit at Market Price", market=true)
```
In this example, we first set up a long entry condition using a crossover of the 20-period and 50-period simple moving averages.

Then, when the long entry condition is met, we enter a long position using the `strategy.entry` function.

Finally, we use an `if` statement to check if we have a long position open and call the `strategy.close` function to close the long position at market price. 

Note that setting `market=true` will close the position at the current market price. You can also specify a specific limit or stop price if you want to close the position at a specific price level.
i wanna indicator not strategy
wht i mean coding is i wanna x = live price of market

I understand. To get the live market price in TradingView Pine Script, you can use the `close` built-in variable. 

In TradingView, `close` represents the current price at which the security is trading. So, to get the current live price in Pine Script, you can simply use:

```
close
```

For example, if you want to use the current market price as the exit price in your code, you can define a variable and assign the `close` value to it as follows:

```
//@version=4
study(title="Exit at Live Price Indicator", overlay=true)

// Set up long entry condition
longCondition = crossover(sma(close, 20), sma(close, 50))

// Enter long position
if (longCondition)
    strategy.entry("Long", strategy.long, comment="Long Entry")

// Exit long position at live price
livePrice = close // Set live price to current market price
if (strategy.position_size > 0 && livePrice >= 100)
    strategy.close("Long", comment="Exit at Live Price", limit=livePrice)
```
In this example, we first set up a long entry condition using a crossover of the 20-period and 50-period simple moving averages.

Then, when the long entry condition is met, we enter a long position using an `if` statement.

Finally, we define a variable called `livePrice` and assign it the value of `close`. This sets `livePrice` to the current market price.

We then use another `if` statement to check if we have a long position open and if `livePrice` is greater than or equal to our exit price. If both conditions are true, we call the `strategy.close` function to exit the long position at the `livePrice`. In this example, we set the limit price to `livePrice` to ensure that we exit at the current market price.
i wanna latest price

To get the latest price in TradingView Pine Script, you can use the `close` built-in variable along with the `barstate.isrealtime` built-in variable. 

In TradingView, `close` represents the current price at which the security is trading. `barstate.isrealtime` returns true when the current bar is still forming (i.e., the current price is not included in the historical price data yet). 

So, to get the latest price in Pine Script, you can use an `if` statement to check if the current bar is still forming. If it is, you can assign an `open`, `high`, `low`, or `close` value to a variable. If it's not, you can assign the `close` value to the variable. Here's an example:

```
//@version=4
study(title="Latest Price", overlay=true)

latestPrice = 0.0 // Initialize latest price variable

if barstate.isrealtime
    latestPrice := open // If current bar is still forming, use the open price
else
    latestPrice := close // If current bar is complete, use the close price

plot(latestPrice, color=color.green, linewidth=2)
```
In this example, we first initialize the `latestPrice` variable to 0.

Then, we use an `if` statement to check if the current bar is still forming using the `barstate.isrealtime` variable. If it is, we set the `latestPrice` to the `open` price. If not, we set it to the `close` price.

Finally, we plot the `latestPrice` variable using the `plot` function.

You can change the variable used in the `if` statement to whatever price level you would like to use as the latest price.

// Calculate
exit = 
pl = ((exit - entry) * qty)
ps = ((exit - entry) * -qty)
pls= deal == 'LONG'?  pl : deal== 'SHORT'? ps : na

It looks like you want to calculate the profit/loss (PL) based on an exit price and a position entry price, along with the quantity (qty) of the position. 

Here's the code snippet to calculate PL using an exit price (as you provided):

```
// Input values
qty = 100 // Change to your desired quantity
entry = 100 // Change to the position entry price
exit = 120 // Change to the exit price

// Calculate profit/loss
pl = ((exit - entry) * qty)
```

In this example, we initialized the `qty` variable to `100`, the `entry` variable to `100`, and the `exit` variable to `120`.

Then, we calculate the profit/loss using the formula `((exit - entry) * qty)`. This calculates the difference between the `entry` and `exit` prices and multiplies that by the `qty` of the position.

You can adjust the `qty`, `entry`, and `exit` variables in the code to your desired values to calculate the PL for your position.

Note that the snippet only calculates profit; we need to modify it for the loss calculation. You can calculate the loss as follows:

```
ps = ((exit - entry) * -qty)
```
In this snippet, we calculate the PL for a short position by multiplying the `(exit - entry)` difference by `-qty`. This converts the result to a negative number, which is what we want for a long position.

Then we can calculate the total PL for a long or short position using this code:

```
pls = deal == 'LONG' ? pl : deal == 'SHORT' ? ps : na
```

Here `pls` represents the total PL which takes into account both long and short positions.

Remember to replace the `deal` value in the above expression - it should contain `LONG` if the position is a long position or `SHORT` if the position is a short position.

// Calculate
exit = 
pl = ((exit - entry) * qty)
ps = ((exit - entry) * -qty)
pls= deal == 'LONG'?  pl : deal== 'SHORT'? ps : na
all i want just exit = market price

If you want the `exit` variable to be the current market price, you can use the `close` variable as we did in the previous examples. 

Here's a modified version of the code where `exit` is set to the current market price:

```
// Input values
qty = 100 // Change to your desired quantity
entry = 100 // Change to the position entry price
exit = close // Use the current market price as the exit price

// Calculate profit/loss
pl = ((exit - entry) * qty)
ps = ((exit - entry) * -qty)
pls = deal == 'LONG' ? pl : deal == 'SHORT' ? ps : na
```

In this modified example, we set `exit` equal to `close`, which represents the current market price. 

Then, we calculate the profit/loss using the same formulas as in the previous example.

You can adjust the `qty` and `entry` variables in the code to your desired values to calculate the PL for your position based on the current market price.
